note: Bambaiya street pronoun; a Hindi dub keeps it as-is for tapori flavour since standard 'main' loses the streetness.
Bambaiya vocative with no clean equivalent; dubs retain it or swap for 'bhai'.
Coded street term for police; literal 'uncle' destroys the meaning, needs glossing as 'police' in dub.
Already a Hindi-Urdu word but used slangily; dub can keep it, register is the only thing lost.
Sound-symbolic Hindi slang; survives untranslated in Hindi dub but has no Tamil cognate, would become 'mass' or 'gethu'.
Classic Bambaiya address; dubs keep it for colour, equivalent to 'yaar' but more street.
Standard Hindi intensifier; carries cleanly, Tamil would use 'romba' or 'mass-aa'.
Iconic Bambaiya exclamation; Hindi dub keeps it, Tamil would use 'semma' or 'kalakkals'.
Vulgar-tinged Hindi slang; meaning ('coward') translates but the crude register doesn't carry to a clean dub.
English word used as Hindi slang; literal English meaning misleads, dub keeps 'item' as loanword.
Mumbai mafia code; literal 'betel nut' is meaningless, must be rendered as 'contract/hit' or kept with context.

actual_meaning: the gang / the boys / underworld crew
note: Compound of bhai+log; dub keeps it, sense of 'crew/gangsters' is recoverable from context.

actual_meaning: worth it / money well spent (paisa vasool)
note: Hindi slang for value-for-money; usually kept as 'vasool', Tamil dub would say 'kaasu vasool'.
Core Tamil slang intensifier; a Hindi dub would replace with 'zabardast/mast', it cannot be carried as a word.
Tamil intensifier; a Hindi dub swaps for 'next level/zabardast', not a literal translation.
Tamil filmi slang for hero swagger; Hindi has no single word, dub would use 'dhansu/dabangg' losing the Tamil cinema connotation.
Tamil slang; Hindi dub would render as 'ekdum local/desi', the 'thara' intensifier and Tamil texture are lost.
English 'scene' + Tamil enunciative -u vowel; Hindi dub drops the -u and says 'kya scene hai', the Tamil suffix is untranslatable.
Tamil sentence-final address particle; no Hindi equivalent, a dub drops it or uses 'be/yaar', flavour is lost entirely.
Feminine counterpart of 'da'; Hindi has no gendered casual particle, dub drops it, the intimacy/gender cue vanishes.
Tamil best-friend address from 'machan'; Hindi dub would use 'bhai/yaar', the kinship-origin street feel doesn't carry.
Tamil slang for cool dominance; Hindi dub approximates with 'swag/rutba', no real cognate exists.
English 'sir' + Tamil -u; often used mockingly, Hindi dub keeps 'sir' but loses the enunciative-vowel sarcasm.
Tamil verb-noun for nailing something; Hindi dub would say 'kya baat/chha gaye', the Tamil idiom isn't portable.

actual_meaning: he's ruining it / causing a mess (Tamil)
note: Tamil colloquial verb; Hindi dub renders as 'gadbad kar raha hai', the specific Tamil verb sense is paraphrased away.
Tamil campus slang ('drinking soup' = dumped); literal English is meaningless, Hindi dub must paraphrase as 'dil tuta aashiq'.

actual_meaning: anglicised show-off / one who flaunts English (Tamil)
note: Tamil slang for an English-pretentious person; no Hindi equivalent, dub would need a descriptive phrase like 'angrezi jhaadne wala'.
Tamil informal word for stench; Hindi dub uses 'badboo/gandh', the playful childish register is lost.
Tamil slang for being calm/silent; Hindi dub approximates with 'chup/shaant', no direct cognate.
Shared with Urdu 'naqal'; Hindi dub can keep 'nakal' so this one actually carries reasonably well.
Tamil slang for flaunting glamour; Hindi dub would use 'nakhre/thaat', the specific Tamil flirt-flaunt sense is approximated.
Tamil internet slang for fan video editors; no Hindi equivalent, dub keeps the English coinage and adds context.
English 'figure' repurposed in Tamil for a girl one fancies; Hindi has its own 'figure' slang so it partly carries but reads filmi.
Sharper than 'da'; Hindi dub would use 'abey/oye', tone carries but the Tamil particle itself can't be translated.

actual_meaning: oh no! / oh dear! (distress or sympathy)
note: Pan-South-Indian interjection; Hindi dub may keep 'aiyo' for South colour or swap to 'arre/haye', widely understood now.
Lengthened 'aiyo' for greater alarm; Hindi dub uses 'haaye re/arre baap', the South Indian colour softens.
English 'super' + Tamil -aa suffix marking question/emphasis; Hindi dub drops the -aa, the Tamil morphology is untranslatable.
Tamil teasing slang for a dull person; Hindi dub would say 'bhondu/buddhu', the Tamil reference is lost.
Hyderabadi slang where 'brinjal' means 'nothing'; literal vegetable misleads, dub needs 'kuch nahi/bekaar'.
Hyderabadi 'haan'; meaning carries fully but the regional pronunciation is the flavour, standard dub flattens it.

actual_meaning: chill / let it go / don't take it seriously (Hyderabadi)
note: Hyderabadi idiom; literal 'take light' is opaque, dub renders as 'chhod na/tension mat le'.
Half-English idiom; Hindi dub keeps 'kya scene hai', carries well across the North too.
Hyderabadi term for annoyance; Hindi dub approximates with 'jhanjhat/lafda', the regional word is dropped.
Hyderabadi casual slang for a girl; Hindi dub would use 'ladki', the local flavour is lost.

actual_meaning: go for it / let's do it / hype call (Hyderabadi)
note: Hyderabadi hype interjection; no Hindi equivalent, dub would use 'chal/shuru ho ja', losing the call-out energy.
Bengali verb famous from protest slogans; Hindi dub renders as 'chalega', sense carries but the Bengali political-cultural weight doesn't.
Bengali kinship address; Hindi 'dada' exists (grandfather) so the dub risks confusion, usually kept with Bengali sense.
Bengali for bhabhi; Hindi dub uses 'bhabhi', meaning carries but the specifically-Bengali social texture is lost.

actual_meaning: superb / great (Bengali borrowing of Mumbai jhakaas)
note: Bengali-adopted exclamation; same as Mumbai 'jhakaas', dub keeps it, Tamil would substitute 'semma'.
Punjabi vocative fully absorbed into Hindi; carries perfectly, Tamil dub would use 'dei/da'.
Punjabi joy exclamation; Hindi keeps it as a loan for festive colour, no real translation, Tamil has no equivalent.

actual_meaning: go for it / let's win / give it your all (Punjabi)
note: Punjabi rallying cry; literal 'lift it' misleads, Hindi dub keeps 'chak de' as a known hype phrase.
Punjabi 'paa ji'; Hindi dub may keep it or use 'bhai sahab', carries but the Punjabi warmth is the flavour.
Punjabi for beautiful; Hindi dub uses 'sundar/pyaari', meaning carries but the Punjabi endearment tone softens.
Shared Hindi-Punjabi word for 'genuine rustic'; carries to Hindi dub, Tamil would paraphrase as 'thara local'.
Kannada cousin of Tamil 'machi'; Hindi dub uses 'bhai/yaar', the kinship-derived street address doesn't carry.

actual_meaning: please make a little adjustment / cooperate a bit (Kannada)
note: Iconic Bangalore phrase; Hindi dub would say 'thoda adjust kar lo', the cultural in-joke and Kannada phrasing are lost.

actual_meaning: completely / very (Kannada intensifier, e.g. full mood)
note: English 'full' used as Kannada intensifier; Hindi dub would use 'ekdum/poora', the Kannada slangy usage is approximated.
Kannada intensifier; Hindi dub swaps for 'zabardast/mast', Tamil would use 'semma', no cross-language word.
Gen-Z loanword used by Indian teens in English; Hindi/Tamil dubs keep it untranslated, any native word sounds dated.

actual_meaning: to do something impressively / nail it (Gen-Z)
note: Gen-Z usage; literal 'kill' misleads, Indian teens keep 'slay' in English, dub leaves it as a loanword.
Gen-Z dating slang; no Hindi/Tamil equivalent, dubs keep 'ick', a literal 'ghin' loses the specific romance sense.
Gen-Z hedge used by Indian teens; Hindi dub might use 'thoda sa/chupke se' but loses the casual English register.

actual_meaning: it has the vibe of / it resembles (Gen-Z)
note: Gen-Z grammatical construction; literal Hindi 'de raha hai' is nonsense, dub must rephrase entirely as 'iska vibe hai'.
Gen-Z internet term; no Hindi/Tamil word captures it, dubs keep 'based' or drop it, literal translation fails.
Gen-Z dismissive; Hindi dub could use 'theek-thaak/average' but the contemptuous brevity is lost.
Gen-Z reduplicative slang; Indian teens keep 'delulu', Hindi 'sapne dekhna' misses the playful self-aware tone.
Gen-Z meme metric ('aura points'); literal 'aura' exists in Hindi but the gamified-cool slang sense doesn't carry, kept in English.

actual_meaning: close buddy / bro (Tamil, full form of machi)
note: Full Tamil form behind 'machi'; Hindi dub uses 'bhai/yaar', the kinship-origin intimacy is untranslatable.
Tamil slang for something that flopped; Hindi dub would say 'phuss/flop ho gaya', the Tamil word has no carry-over.
Tamil insult that can be playful; Hindi dub uses 'kameena/badmaash', tone carries but the Tamil word is dropped.
English 'loose' + Tamil -u; Hindi dub would use 'paagal/sanki', the Tamil enunciative-vowel coinage can't be translated.

actual_meaning: create drama / show off / make a move (Tamil)
note: Tamil verb-phrase idiom; literal 'put scene' is opaque, Hindi dub renders as 'drama mat kar/scene mat bana'.
Bambaiya slang for corner-bench loiterers; Hindi dub keeps 'katta panchayat' as colour or paraphrases 'gali ki baithak'.
Bambaiya word now mainstream Hindi; dub keeps 'bindaas', the meaning is widely understood, Tamil would say 'gammu-aa'.
Bambaiya slang for excellent; Hindi dub keeps it or uses 'mast', Tamil would swap for 'kalakkals'.
Bambaiya filler meaning 'pointlessly'; Hindi dub uses 'bekaar mein/aise hi', the street word is dropped.
Bambaiya for a snag; Hindi dub keeps 'locha' (fairly mainstream) or uses 'gadbad', carries okay.

actual_meaning: messing around / making a mess (Bambaiya joke slang)
note: Bambaiya playful mock-gerund; impossible to translate literally, dub paraphrases as 'gadbad karna'.
Bambaiya slang; literal 'rag' misleads, Hindi dub uses 'kanjoos/ghatiya', Tamil would say 'thara local'.
Pan-Hindi concept now global; dub keeps 'jugaad', Tamil also borrows it, the cultural concept is hard to translate cleanly.
Indian campus slang; Hindi dub keeps 'funda', understood pan-India, Tamil also uses it.
Indian English slang for an arrangement (e.g. 'setting hai'); Hindi dub keeps 'setting', literal misleads.
Indian-English loan for accommodating; Hindi dub keeps 'adjust', Kannada 'adjust maadi' is the famous form.
Pan-Indian coinage; Hindi dub keeps 'timepass', widely understood, Tamil also uses it.

actual_meaning: boss / the GOAT (Tamil, often for a star)
note: Tamil for leader, iconic fan-address for Rajinikanth; Hindi dub uses 'boss/sardar', the Tamil fandom weight is lost.
Tamil for friend; Hindi dub uses 'dost/yaar', meaning carries but the Tamil warmth and address-form are softened.

actual_meaning: get lost / go on (Tamil, dismissive or casual)
note: Tamil dismissive imperative; Hindi dub uses 'jaao/chalo hato', the Tamil verb conjugation can't carry over.

actual_meaning: what's up / what is it, dude (Tamil)
note: Tamil 'enna' + 'da'; Hindi dub renders as 'kya hua be', the 'da' particle and Tamil cadence are lost.
Tamil slang for showy; Hindi dub would use 'dhinchak/bhadkila', no cognate, the Tamil colour is dropped.
Shared with Urdu 'ghaleez'; Hindi dub can keep 'galeez', so this one carries fairly well across both.
Tamil 'chumma' (just/idly) is a false friend with Hindi 'chumma' (kiss); a literal Hindi dub would be badly misread, must paraphrase 'aise hi'.
Tamil for shame; Hindi dub uses 'sharam', meaning carries cleanly, only the Tamil word itself is swapped.
English 'super' + Tamil 'dei'; Hindi dub says 'mast be/zabardast yaar', the 'dei' particle is untranslatable.
English 'local' + Tamil -u; Hindi dub drops the -u and says 'local/ghatiya', the suffix-as-flavour is lost.
English 'vibe' + Tamil enunciative -u; Hindi dub uses plain 'vibe', losing the characteristic Tamil vowel-ending.

actual_meaning: forget it / drop it / blow it off (Mumbai)
note: Bambaiya idiom for 'ditch it'; literal 'shoot a bullet' misleads, Hindi dub keeps 'goli maar' (mainstream) for 'chhod de'.
Filmi Hindi-Punjabi slang; literal 'shiny girl' is close but the playful flirty register needs the original, kept as a phrase.
Hindi slang for 'out of the box'; dub keeps 'hatke', Tamil would render as 'vera maari', the idiom doesn't translate word-for-word.
Hindi slang for triumph; literal weather sense misleads, Tamil dub would use 'kalakkals/gethu'.
Urdu-origin Hindi slang for being smitten; carries in Hindi, Tamil dub would paraphrase 'mayangiten/figure mela'.

actual_meaning: to make a mess of things / botch it up (Hindi)
note: Hindi idiom; literal 'spread raita' is meaningless in Tamil, dub must paraphrase as 'kuzhappam panniten' (made a mess).
